Mild.summary: Early anthracnose symptoms are visible on a limited portion of leaves, flowers, or young fruit, and disease spread is still relatively localized.
treatment: Remove and safely discard visibly infected twigs, leaves, flowers, or fruit to reduce inoculum pressure.
Improve canopy airflow through light pruning and avoid conditions that prolong leaf and flower wetness.
Where locally approved and appropriate, apply preventative fungicidal protection to healthy tissues as part of an integrated management program.
For postharvest fruit protection, validated biological or physical postharvest treatments may be considered where available.
prevention: Monitor closely during humid, rainy, or prolonged wet periods because these conditions favor infection and disease development.
Reduce canopy density where practical to lower humidity and improve drying.
Avoid practices that increase splash dispersal or prolonged surface moisture.
monitoring: Inspect again within 5 to 7 days during humid conditions.
Check whether symptoms are spreading to flowers, panicles, or developing fruit.
caution: Anthracnose management is more effective when started early rather than after extensive spread.
No single cultivar is fully resistant across all known anthracnose-causing strains.

Moderate.summary: Disease is established on a noticeable part of the canopy or fruiting structures, with increased risk of fruit set loss and postharvest quality reduction.
treatment: Prune and remove infected plant material more thoroughly to reduce ongoing inoculum.
Use integrated management combining sanitation, canopy management, and preventative protection of healthy tissue where locally approved.
During flowering and fruit set, pay particular attention to disease suppression because infection at these stages can strongly affect fruit development.
For harvested fruit, validated postharvest management options may be used where available to reduce lesion development during storage and handling.
prevention: Reduce humidity within the canopy through pruning and spacing where feasible.
Remove fallen infected debris and other sources of persistent inoculum.
Plan management around weather conditions, especially wet periods that increase infection risk.
monitoring: Inspect every 3 to 5 days during favorable disease periods.
Track whether lesions are increasing on flowers, panicles, or fruit.
caution: Anthracnose can remain latent and become more obvious later, including after harvest.
Relying only on late-stage intervention is less effective than integrated early management.

Severe.summary: Anthracnose is widespread, with extensive infection affecting foliage, flowers, and/or fruit, and there is high risk of major preharvest and postharvest loss.
treatment: Remove heavily infected plant material promptly to reduce disease pressure and protect any remaining healthy tissue.
Use a full integrated management strategy combining sanitation, canopy opening, and validated preventative protection measures where locally approved.
Where fruit is harvested, apply only validated postharvest management approaches that are supported by available evidence and local guidance.
Focus management on protecting remaining healthy tissue and limiting further spread rather than attempting to recover severely damaged organs.
prevention: Maintain strict orchard sanitation and remove infected debris from the production area.
For future planting or replacement, prefer cultivars reported to be less susceptible where suitable local options exist.
Strengthen monitoring during flowering, fruit set, and extended wet weather.
monitoring: Inspect frequently during active outbreaks and monitor neighboring mango trees for spread.
Monitor harvested fruit closely for postharvest lesion expansion.
caution: Severe mango anthracnose can substantially reduce both yield and postharvest market quality.
Because latent infections are common, symptom severity before harvest may underestimate postharvest risk.

Mild.summary: Early powdery mildew symptoms are visible on a limited portion of leaves, flowers, or young tissues, and infection is still localized.
treatment: Remove and safely discard visibly infected tissues where infection is still limited.
Improve canopy airflow through light pruning and by avoiding excessive canopy density.
Where locally approved and appropriate, apply sulfur-based protective treatment as part of an integrated management approach.
Begin management early, especially during flowering and early fruit development, because these stages are highly vulnerable.
prevention: Monitor closely during conditions favorable for powdery mildew development, especially around flowering and young fruit stages.
Reduce prolonged humidity within the canopy where possible.
Avoid practices that keep young tissues damp for long periods.
monitoring: Inspect the tree again within 5 to 7 days during favorable weather.
Check whether white powdery growth is spreading to panicles, flowers, or young fruit.
caution: Powdery mildew can significantly reduce fruit set if not managed early.
Sulfur-based products should only be used according to locally approved guidance and label instructions.

Moderate.summary: Disease is established on a noticeable part of the canopy or reproductive structures, with increasing risk to flowering, fruit set, and yield.
treatment: Prune and remove infected plant material more thoroughly to reduce inoculum pressure.
Use an integrated management strategy combining sanitation, canopy opening, and protective sulfur-based management where locally approved.
Pay particular attention to disease suppression during flowering and fruit set, since these stages are strongly affected by powdery mildew.
prevention: Reduce canopy humidity and improve air movement through pruning and spacing where possible.
Monitor susceptible growth flushes, flowers, and young fruit regularly.
For future planting decisions, prefer cultivars reported to be relatively less susceptible where locally suitable.
monitoring: Inspect every 3 to 5 days during active disease-conducive periods.
Track whether infection is spreading to additional panicles, flowers, or fruit.

Severe.summary: Powdery mildew is widespread, with extensive infection affecting flowers, young fruit, and/or new vegetative growth, and there is high risk of severe fruit set reduction.
treatment: Remove heavily infected plant material where practical to reduce ongoing inoculum.
Use a full integrated management approach combining sanitation, canopy management, and validated protective treatment where locally approved.
Focus on protecting remaining healthy flowers, young fruit, and new growth rather than attempting to recover severely damaged tissues.

Strengthen monitoring during flowering and early fruit development, which are critical disease-sensitive stages.
Consider varietal susceptibility when selecting planting material for future seasons.

treatment: Remove and safely discard visibly infected plant parts where infection is still limited.
Apply locally approved protectant treatments to healthy foliage as part of an integrated management program.
Where available, consider biological management options supported by evidence, such as selected Bacillus-based products or bacteriophage-based suppression strategies.

Do not handle or prune plants when foliage is wet.

Early intervention is more effective than waiting until infection becomes widespread.

Severe.summary: Bacterial spot is widespread, with extensive lesions on foliage and/or fruit and high risk of major plant damage and further spread.
treatment: Remove severely infected plants or heavily affected plant material to reduce inoculum pressure and protect nearby plants.
Use a full integrated management strategy focused on sanitation, splash reduction, clean planting material, and locally approved protective measures.
Plan future control around host resistance, clean seed/transplants, and stronger preventative management rather than relying only on in-season treatment.
prevention: Remove all infected crop debris at the end of the season.
Use disease-free seed or validated seed sanitation procedures before future planting.
Prefer resistant or less susceptible cultivars where locally suitable options exist.
monitoring: Inspect surrounding susceptible plants frequently during active outbreaks.
Monitor new flushes of growth and fruit for continued lesion development.

treatment: Remove and safely discard visibly infected lower leaves where infection is still limited.
Apply locally approved protectant fungicidal treatment to healthy foliage as part of an integrated management program.

Begin protection early rather than waiting for extensive lesion development.
monitoring: Inspect the crop again within 5 to 7 days under favorable disease conditions.

Use integrated management combining locally approved protectant fungicides, cultural control, and supportive biological options where available.

treatment: Remove heavily infected plant material where practical to reduce inoculum pressure.
Use a full integrated management strategy focused on protecting remaining healthy foliage through validated local control measures.
Prioritize slowing further spread rather than attempting to recover severely damaged leaves.

Mild.summary: Early late blight symptoms are visible on a limited portion of foliage, typically as water-soaked dark lesions, but disease spread is still relatively localized.
treatment: Remove and safely discard visibly infected foliage where disease is still limited.
Apply locally approved preventative protection effective against oomycete pathogens to protect healthy foliage.
Strengthen crop protection immediately if cool, wet, or foggy weather is expected.

Water at the base of the plant where possible and avoid practices that increase splash dispersal.
Monitor nearby potato and tomato plants because linked spread can occur under favorable conditions.

Check whether lesions are expanding or whether pale or white sporulation appears around lesion margins.

Because Phytophthora infestans is an oomycete rather than a true fungus, management should rely on measures validated for oomycete control.

monitoring: Inspect surrounding tomato plants and nearby susceptible hosts frequently during active outbreaks.
Continue monitoring whitefly abundance even after removing symptomatic plants.
caution: Severe TYLCV outbreaks can cause major yield loss and are difficult to reverse once widely established.
Long-term management depends more on resistance and vector control than on treatment after symptom expression.

references.title: The viral etiology of tomato yellow leaf curl disease - a review
Natural resistance of tomato plants to Tomato yellow leaf curl virus
Integrated Management of Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus and Bemisia tabaci in Tomato Using Resistant Tomato Cultivars, Insecticides, and Mulches

Mango_Healthy.summary: The leaf appears healthy, with no major visible disease pattern detected.
treatment: No disease treatment is required at this stage.
prevention: Maintain good canopy airflow through appropriate pruning.
Monitor the tree during humid and rainy periods when fungal diseases are more likely to develop.
Avoid prolonged leaf wetness where possible.
monitoring: Inspect the tree again within 7 to 14 days.
Reassess if spots, powdery growth, or fruit lesions appear.
caution: A healthy-looking leaf can still develop symptoms later, especially under favorable disease conditions.

references.title: General orchard sanitation and disease prevention practices for mango production

Pepper_Healthy.summary: The leaf appears healthy, with no major visible disease pattern detected.
treatment: No disease treatment is required at this stage.
prevention: Avoid overhead irrigation where possible to reduce bacterial and fungal spread.
Use clean tools and avoid handling foliage when wet.
Maintain good spacing and airflow between plants.
monitoring: Inspect the plant again within 5 to 7 days.
Reassess if water-soaked spots, yellow halos, or new lesions appear.
caution: Healthy leaves can still become infected later under warm, wet, or splash-prone conditions.

references.title: General disease prevention and sanitation practices for pepper production

Potato_Healthy.summary: The leaf appears healthy, with no major visible disease pattern detected.
treatment: No disease treatment is required at this stage.
prevention: Reduce soil splash by maintaining mulch or appropriate crop spacing where practical.
Avoid prolonged leaf wetness and unnecessary overhead irrigation.
Monitor frequently during cool, wet, or humid periods that favor blight development.
monitoring: Inspect the crop again within 5 to 7 days.
Reassess if target-like lesions, water-soaked spots, or rapid canopy changes appear.
caution: Potato foliage can appear healthy before rapid disease onset under favorable weather conditions.

references.title: General potato disease prevention and field sanitation practices

Tomato_Healthy.summary: The leaf appears healthy, with no major visible disease pattern detected.
treatment: No disease treatment is required at this stage.
prevention: Maintain good airflow and avoid excessive canopy density.
Reduce soil splash with mulch or careful watering practices.
Avoid prolonged leaf wetness and inspect regularly during humid conditions.
monitoring: Inspect the plant again within 5 to 7 days.
Reassess if spots, mold, curling, yellowing, or water-soaked lesions appear.
caution: Healthy leaves can still develop disease later, especially under favorable environmental conditions or vector pressure.

references.title: General tomato disease prevention and crop hygiene practices
